**Nicolae Constantin Dică** (Romanian pronunciation: [[nikoˈla.e konstanˈtin ˈdikə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian"); born 9 May 1980) is a Romanian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [manager](/wiki/Manager_(association_football) "Manager (association football)") and former player.

Deployed as an [attacking midfielder](/wiki/Attacking_midfielder "Attacking midfielder") or a [forward](/wiki/Forward_(association_football) "Forward (association football)"), Dică was known for his incisive passing, long balls and spectacular finishes. He played professionally in four countries, but spent most of his career in his native Romania representing [Dacia Mioveni](/wiki/CS_Mioveni "CS Mioveni"), [Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești"), [Steaua București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB"), [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") and [Viitorul Constanța](/wiki/FC_Viitorul_Constan%C8%9Ba "FC Viitorul Constanța"). He was a [Romanian international](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") for seven years, and was selected for the [UEFA Euro 2008](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2008 "UEFA Euro 2008").

Since his retirement as a player, Dică has had several stints as either an assistant, a caretaker or a head coach at his former club Steaua București. He also managed Argeș Pitești and was an assistant at the Romania national team.

## Club career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Club career")]

### Dacia Pitești

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Dacia Pitești")]

Dică started playing professional football when he was 18 years old at a local team [Dacia Pitești](/wiki/FC_Dacia_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Dacia Pitești").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In his first season, Dică played in 17 games and scored 5 times for Dacia. In the second, he became a first team player and had a very good season, with Dacia finishing fourth in the [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B"). He was one of the top scorers in the league that season with 14 goals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Argeș Pitești

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Argeș Pitești")]

Dică was seen as an upcoming talent and the local [Divizia A](/wiki/Divizia_A "Divizia A") club, [Argeș Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești"), signed him in 2000.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He made his debut for Argeș Pitești in a 2–1 win against [Gaz Metan Mediaș](/wiki/CS_Gaz_Metan_Media%C8%99 "CS Gaz Metan Mediaș").[[4]](#cite_note-4) He was soon awarded the captain's armband after the transfers of [Adrian Mutu](/wiki/Adrian_Mutu "Adrian Mutu") to [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") and [Adrian Neaga](/wiki/Adrian_Neaga "Adrian Neaga") to [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București").

### Steaua București

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Steaua București")]

Romanian giants [Steaua București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB") paid €250,000 to sign him from Argeș Pitești in December 2003. He was transferred to Steaua after the recommendation of [Victor Pițurcă](/wiki/Victor_Pi%C8%9Burc%C4%83 "Victor Pițurcă") and [Mihai Stoica](/wiki/Mihai_Stoica "Mihai Stoica").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

He made his debut in Europe in the [2004–05 UEFA Cup](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_UEFA_Cup "2004–05 UEFA Cup") season, when Steaua eliminated previous winners [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF"), en route to the [Round of 16](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_UEFA_Cup_final_phase "2004–05 UEFA Cup final phase"), where they were knocked out by another Spanish side [Villarreal](/wiki/Villarreal_CF "Villarreal CF").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 2005 after Dică lost the chance to play in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), due to Steaua being eliminated by [Rosenborg BK](/wiki/Rosenborg_BK "Rosenborg BK"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) he had another successful season in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"). The Bucharest club defeated the likes of [Lens](/wiki/RC_Lens "RC Lens"), [Heereenven](/wiki/SC_Heerenveen "SC Heerenveen"), [Real Betis](/wiki/Real_Betis "Real Betis") and their Romanian rivals [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) on their way to the UEFA CUP semi-final, where they were eliminated by [English Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") club, [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_FC "Middlesbrough FC").[[9]](#cite_note-Boro_win_4-3_on_aggregate-9)

On 13 September 2006, Dică scored twice in the 4–1 away win against [Dynamo Kyiv](/wiki/FC_Dynamo_Kyiv "FC Dynamo Kyiv") in Steaua București's opening game in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), this being his first ever appearance in said tournament.[[10]](#cite_note-Steaua_Thrashed_Dynamo_Kiev_in_Champions_League_Opener-10) He went on to score four goals and add one assist in six games against group opponents, Dynamo Kyiv, [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid "Real Madrid"), and [Lyon](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais"). After his performances in the Champions League, the daily newspaper *[Gazeta Sporturilor](/wiki/Gazeta_Sporturilor "Gazeta Sporturilor")* gave the [Romanian Footballer of the Year 2006](/wiki/Romanian_Footballer_of_the_Year "Romanian Footballer of the Year") award to Dică.[[11]](#cite_note-Nicolae_Dica_este_jucătorul_anului_2006_în_Ancheta_Gazetei-11)

On 23 January 2007, he suffered a [knee injury](/wiki/Knee_injury "Knee injury") while playing in a friendly against [AC Siena](/wiki/S.S._Robur_Siena "S.S. Robur Siena"), and missed most of the season.[[12]](#cite_note-Dică_riscă_să_piardă_tot_sezonul-12)
On 9 May, for his birthday, Dică returned to the team and was introduced in the late stages of the 2–0 victory against [Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț](/wiki/CSM_Ceahl%C4%83ul_Piatra_Neam%C8%9B "CSM Ceahlăul Piatra Neamț").[[13]](#cite_note-13) This was his first official game in 2007.

### Catania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Catania")]

On 28 June 2008, Dică moved to [Catania](/wiki/Calcio_Catania "Calcio Catania"), mainly due to the desire of his former Steaua coach, [Walter Zenga](/wiki/Walter_Zenga "Walter Zenga"). He was expected to replace midfielder [Juan Manuel Vargas](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_Vargas "Juan Manuel Vargas") who moved to [ACF Fiorentina](/wiki/ACF_Fiorentina "ACF Fiorentina"),[[14]](#cite_note-Catania_Snap_Up_Nicolae_Dica-14) but ended up being a flop, taking part in just 93 minutes of play during his first six months with Palermo. Although it seemed almost certain that he would leave during the January transfer market, coach Walter Zenga kept him at Catania until the end of the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") season.

In June 2009, [Iraklis](/wiki/Iraklis_F.C._(Thessaloniki) "Iraklis F.C. (Thessaloniki)") signed Dică on loan from [Calcio Catania](/wiki/Calcio_Catania "Calcio Catania").[[15]](#cite_note-15) He made his competitive debut on 23 August 2009 and scored twice in a 2–1 win over [Panthrakikos](/wiki/Panthrakikos_F.C. "Panthrakikos F.C.").

In January 2010, [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") signed the midfielder on loan from Catania until June 2010. He won the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") and the [Romanian Cup](/wiki/Romanian_Cup "Romanian Cup") with them, scoring his first goal in a 2–1 victory against Dinamo București in the second leg of the Cup semi-final.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In July 2010, [Manisaspor](/wiki/Manisaspor "Manisaspor") signed the midfielder on loan from Catania until June 2011. He rarely made it to the first team and then, on 14 December 2010, he agreed to cancel his contract with Manisaspor.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later years")]

In January 2011, Dică signed a one-year contract with his former team, [Steaua București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB").[[17]](#cite_note-17) He made his debut for the club, on 27 February 2011, in a 1–0 win at [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/FC_Universitatea_Craiova "FC Universitatea Craiova").[[18]](#cite_note-18) On 5 April, he scored his first goal after his return to Steaua in a 5–0 home win over [Unirea Urziceni](/wiki/FC_Unirea_Urziceni "FC Unirea Urziceni"). On 11 May, Dică scored the equalizer against [AS Brașov](/wiki/AS_SR_Bra%C8%99ov "AS SR Brașov"), which sent Steaua through to the [Romanian Cup](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2010–11 Cupa României") final. On 25 May, Dică scored from a trademark free kick in a 2–1 victory over rivals Dinamo București, and won his second consecutive Romanian cup.

In May 2011, Dică joined [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") side [Dacia Mioveni](/wiki/CS_Mioveni "CS Mioveni"). He received the number 80 jersey.[[19]](#cite_note-19) On 31 October, Dică scored a goal in a 3–1 defeat to [Concordia Chiajna](/wiki/CS_Concordia_Chiajna "CS Concordia Chiajna").[[20]](#cite_note-20) In January 2012, after fifteen games for the Liga I squad, he terminated his contract with Mioveni.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Later that month he signed a one-and-a-half-year contract with [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") side [Viitorul Constanța](/wiki/FC_Viitorul_Constan%C8%9Ba "FC Viitorul Constanța").[[22]](#cite_note-22) At the end of the [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Liga_II "2011–12 Liga II") season, Viitorul earned promotion to the Liga I,[[23]](#cite_note-23) in part due to Dică's performances, who scored six goals in the second half of the season.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## International career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Ndica.jpg/500px-Ndica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ndica.jpg)

Dică playing against the [Nigeria national team](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team")

On 11 October 2003, Dică made his debut for the [Romania national team](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") in a friendly game against [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team").[[25]](#cite_note-25) He scored his first goal in August 2006, in a friendly game against [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_national_football_team "Cyprus national football team") and amassed a total of 32 appearances for Romania.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

On 15 May 2017, it was announced that Nicolae Dică will be the manager of [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB") for the [2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Liga_I "2017–18 Liga I") season.[[27]](#cite_note-27) He went undefeated in his first ten games in all competitions before losing to Portuguese club [Sporting CP](/wiki/Sporting_C.P. "Sporting C.P."), in the [2017–18 Champions League play-off](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_UEFA_Champions_League_qualifying_phase_and_play-off_round "2017–18 UEFA Champions League qualifying phase and play-off round").[[28]](#cite_note-28) In December 2018, after a year and a half at the helm of the club, Dică terminated his contract with FCSB by mutual consent.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

On 26 July 2022, Dică returned to FCSB.[[30]](#cite_note-30) On 1 November 2022, Dică terminated his contract with FCSB by mutual consent.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

On 14 January 2023, he was presented as the new manager of his former club [Mioveni](/wiki/CS_Mioveni "CS Mioveni").[[33]](#cite_note-33) He left the team by mutual consent on 26 April 2023.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

On 17 July that year, he took charge of another Liga I side [FC U Craiova](/wiki/FC_U_Craiova_1948 "FC U Craiova 1948").[[35]](#cite_note-35) After two losses in the opening games of the season, he recorded two wins, before suffering a 4–3 defeat to [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești") on 18 August. The following day, only 34 days into his tenure, he was dismissed from the club.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicolae_Dic%C4%83&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life")]

In November 2003, Dică married Corina Zimbroianu, after the two met in 1998. He has a son, Marco Ilie, who was baptized by his former Steaua teammate, [Mirel Radoi](/wiki/Mirel_Radoi "Mirel Radoi").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In March 2007, he had a difficult period in his life as his father died due to [cirrhosis](/wiki/Cirrhosis "Cirrhosis"). Dică was under treatment at the Isokinetic clinic, in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), when he heard the news.[[38]](#cite_note-38) His father was the one who encouraged him to become a footballer.
